expectations.text: Identifies the cartesian/row-explosion risk from the 1:N joins (order_item and payment) and suggests separate queries or aggregation
expectations.evidence: Finding 2: 8x2=16 near-identical rows; fix = page orders then batch-load children via ANY($1), or aggregate with LATERAL

expectations.text: Flags DATE(o.created_at) as a function/cast on the column that disables the index, and rewrites it as a range on the raw column
expectations.evidence: Finding 1: DATE() forces per-row compute, index unusable; rewrite created_at >= '2025-01-01 00:00:00'

expectations.text: Flags SELECT * and recommends selecting only the needed columns
expectations.evidence: Finding 5: SELECT * across four tables, blocks index-only scan; use explicit column list

expectations.text: Flags the high OFFSET (80000) and recommends keyset/cursor pagination
expectations.evidence: Finding 3: OFFSET 80000 reads+discards 80k rows; keyset on (created_at,id)

expectations.text: States a tradeoff for at least one recommendation (not just the fix)
expectations.evidence: Every finding has explicit Tradeoff; e.g. keyset can't jump to arbitrary page; index taxes writes

expectations.text: Provides at least one corrected SQL example
expectations.evidence: Multiple corrected SQL blocks (range filter, batched children, keyset, index)

expectations.text: Explicitly identifies the N+1 problem as the cause
expectations.evidence: Verdict: 'textbook N+1 query problem' (smell #3)

expectations.text: Explains that the loop produces roughly one query per item (order of N+1 round trips)
expectations.evidence: 1 + 200 + 200 = 401 round trips for a page of 200

expectations.text: Recommends loading related rows in a single/batched query (IN / ANY / eager load) instead of per-item
expectations.evidence: Option A: In(ids) batched + stitch -> 3 queries; Option B relations

expectations.text: States a tradeoff or caveat for the recommended fix
expectations.evidence: Tradeoff: app-side stitching maps; Option B trusts ORM strategy, must verify SQL

expectations.text: Recognizes audit_event as a transactional / continuously-growing table
expectations.evidence: 'This is a transactional table... append-only history that grows without bound'

expectations.text: Recommends an indexed date filter / bounded time window for queries on it
expectations.evidence: Articulates smell #4: 'a query on this table without a time window will read history that only grows; the time filter keeps work proportional'; pattern (b) keeps created_at bound on indexed col

expectations.text: Proposes composite indexes for the stated access patterns and addresses column order (e.g. (user_id, created_at), (type, created_at))
expectations.evidence: (user_id, created_at DESC) and (type, created_at DESC) with explicit equality-leads-range column-order reasoning

expectations.text: Mentions the write/storage cost of indexes or warns against over-indexing (a tradeoff)
expectations.evidence: 'Index restraint... every index taxes the write path, and this is a write-heavy table'

expectations.text: Identifies the NOT IN + NULL trap (empty result if subquery yields a NULL)
expectations.evidence: Section 1: explains three-valued logic, 'returns empty result set, no error'

expectations.text: Recommends NOT EXISTS (or an equivalent NULL-safe construct)
expectations.evidence: Provides NOT EXISTS rewrite; notes alternative NOT NULL guarantee

expectations.text: Does NOT treat SELECT * or a missing date filter as a real performance problem here, acknowledging the table is small/static (context overrides the rule)
expectations.evidence: 'performance angle is a non-issue at ~80 static rows'; SELECT * called a 'maintainability nit, not performance'
